title: Pay up or not? Ransomware surge has victims facing tough choices.
excerpt: Governments look at banning ransom payments in face of increasingly sophisticated threats.
content_html:
Nearly half of companies that are targets of a ransomware cyber attack end up paying a ransom to release their data or systems, according to 2025 research from cybersecurity group Sophos, while the median amount demanded is rising.

Globally, some jurisdictions are responding by banning payments to hackers. In the UK, for example, the government is advancing plans to prohibit public sector bodies and critical national infrastructure groups—including the National Health Service, local councils and schools—from making payouts.

The potential veto comes as ransomware hackers have become more advanced and meticulous in their targeting of companies, particularly vulnerable small and medium-sized businesses, over time.

“In 2026, the ransomware landscape has evolved into a highly sophisticated, corporate-style ecosystem,” says Haydn Brooks, chief executive of supply chain security group Risk Ledger. “While ransomware groups operate like smart B2B operations to ensure data return, the legal and sanction risks of paying are at an all-time high.”

This has been powered by the rise of malicious AI hacking tools such as WormGPT, FraudGPT and BruteForceAI, according to Dave Spillane, systems engineering director at Fortinet, who notes that confirmed ransomware victims rose 389 percent year-on-year in 2025, from around 1,600 in 2024 to 7,831 globally.

“In the time it would have previously taken to commit one ransomware attack, hackers can now target four separate organizations simultaneously,” he says.

“The cost per attack has dramatically decreased, commoditizing sophisticated attacks, whereas the cost to defend is increasing,” agrees Shashi Kiran, chief marketing officer of tech group Nile. “What required nation states earlier can be accomplished by individuals with half-baked skills leveraging the power of AI.”

Nevertheless, whether to pay out or not remains one of the most divisive areas in cybersecurity.

Jim Walter, a senior threat researcher at SentinelOne, says that his cyber security group takes a hard line against responding to ransoms.

“Paying extortive threat actors only strengthens the ecosystem and the entities that enable it,” he says, noting that threat actors cannot be trusted to delete data upon payment.

Re-extortion and the ongoing monetization of stolen data are commonplace, he adds. “Paying absolutely does not guarantee recovery, it actually encourages further crime and extortion.”

Others are less absolute. “Our concern with a ban is what happens when a payment ban is in place but data recovery is not feasible,” says Andy Maus, head of cyber recovery services at DriveSavers, which recovers hard drive data. “Situations are almost always more nuanced than a ban accounts for.”

sentences.zh: “我们对禁令的担忧是，当支付禁令到位但数据恢复不可行时会发生什么，”恢复硬盘数据的 DriveSavers 网络恢复服务主管 Andy Maus 表示。

content_html_zh:
根据网络安全组织 Sophos 2025 年的研究，近一半成为勒索软件网络攻击目标的公司最终会支付赎金以释放其数据或系统，而要求的中位数正在上升。

在全球范围内，一些司法管辖区正在采取措施，禁止向黑客付款。 例如，在英国，政府正在推进计划，禁止公共部门机构和重要的国家基础设施团体（包括国家医疗服务体系、地方议会和学校）支付费用。

随着时间的推移，勒索软件黑客在针对公司，特别是脆弱的中小型企业的目标方面变得更加先进和细致，因此可能会出现否决权。

供应链安全组织 Risk Ledger 首席执行官海顿·布鲁克斯 (Haydn Brooks) 表示：“到 2026 年，勒索软件格局已演变成一个高度复杂的企业式生态系统。” “虽然勒索软件组织像智能 B2B 操作一样运作以确保数据返回，但支付的法律和制裁风险却处于历史最高水平。”

Fortinet 系统工程总监 Dave Spillane 表示，这得益于 WormGPT、FraudGPT 和 BruteForceAI 等恶意 AI 黑客工具的兴起，他指出，2025 年已确认的勒索软件受害者同比增长 389%，从 2024 年的约 1,600 人增加到全球 7,831 人。

“以前发起一次勒索软件攻击所需的时间，现在黑客可以同时针对四个不同的组织，”他说。

“每次攻击的成本已大幅下降，复杂的攻击已商品化，而防御成本却在增加，”科技集团 Nile 的首席营销官 Shashi Kiran 表示同意。 “早期需要民族国家实现的任务，可以由技术不成熟的个人利用人工智能的力量来完成。”

尽管如此，是否支付仍然是网络安全领域最具分歧的领域之一。

SentinelOne 的高级威胁研究员吉姆·沃尔特 (Jim Walter) 表示，他的网络安全团队对勒索赎金采取强硬立场。

他表示：“向勒索性威胁行为者付费只会增强生态系统和支持该生态系统的实体。”他指出，不能相信威胁行为者会在付款后删除数据。

他补充说，重新勒索和不断将被盗数据货币化是很常见的。 “支付绝对不能保证康复，它实际上会鼓励进一步的犯罪和敲诈勒索。”

其他的则不那么绝对。 “我们对禁令的担忧是，当支付禁令到位但数据恢复不可行时会发生什么，”恢复硬盘数据的 DriveSavers 网络恢复服务主管 Andy Maus 表示。 “情况几乎总是比禁令所解释的更加微妙。”
